findings.description: reachable() never uses its `seen` set as a visited-guard, so on a cyclic graph (which the docstring explicitly permits) `queue.append(neighbor)` enqueues forever — non-terminating loop and unbounded memory growth; even on a DAG, nodes reachable via k paths are re-expanded k times, making work exponential in graph depth. Fix: only `append`/`add` when `neighbor not in seen`.

count_paths() recursively recomputes count_paths(neighbor, dst) with no memoization, so on a diamond/lattice DAG the call count blows up to ~O(2^depth) (classic un-memoized recursion); memoize on src (dst is fixed per call) or use lru_cache to drop it to O(V+E). Secondary: unbounded recursion depth risks a stack overflow on deep graphs.

add_node() emits logger.info per registration; in a bulk data-pipeline load of millions of nodes the per-call logging dispatch/handler overhead adds up — consider DEBUG level or batched logging (lazy %s formatting already keeps the string cost low, so this is borderline).

neighbors() returns a fresh list(self._edges[node]) defensive copy on every call (O(degree)); intentional and standard, but if called in a hot loop callers could be handed the internal list view instead — micro-optimization only.
